title: The drain is bullying again
intro: Scrub the drain voltage and watch the hilltop sag — that is DIBL, drain-induced barrier lowering: the drain's potential reaching in and stealing the gate's barrier. At 340 mV/V your planar device's threshold basically belongs to the drain, and the off-state crowd pours over. A longer gate alone won't save you at these dimensions — wrap the terrain's walls instead: change the architecture. Get DIBL under 50 mV/V with a sub-20 nm gate.
explain: This single problem drove the whole transistor roadmap: planar → FinFET (Intel, 22 nm, 2011) → gate-all-around nanosheets (Samsung 3 nm, TSMC N2). Each step gates the channel from more sides — one, three, then all four — shrinking the natural length λ the drain field needs to invade. On the terrain you saw it as walls: the more sides the gate fences, the less the drain can reach in and melt the hilltop. Same dimensions, different geometry: your GAA device has ~10× less DIBL than the planar one.

choices: The hilltop stays put — it's the gate's job
The hilltop sinks as the drain pulls harder
The hilltop rises to fight back

prediction.prompt: You only raise the DRAIN voltage. What happens to the barrier the GATE is supposed to own?
